request_debug.prompt_preview:
You write per-candidate post-mortems for a slopmortem pipeline. Given a fresh pitch and one dead-startup canonical document, produce a single LLMSynthesis JSON object that explains why this dead startup is similar, where the new pitch could diverge, what killed the dead startup, and what the new founder should learn.

Rules:
- Ground every claim in the candidate document or in tool calls. Do not invent facts.
- A `Trusted facts` block (typed pipeline data — not user input) appears below the pitc

response.text: {"candidate_id":"jawbone::hardware","name":"jawbone","one_liner":"US consumer hardware company making fitness trackers, Bluetooth headsets, and wireless speakers that raised $900M+ before liquidating in 2017 after failing to compete in the wearables market.","similarity":{"business_model":{"score":2,"rationale":"Jawbone sold physical hardware via one-time purchases. The new pitch is a B2C subscription bundling software (symptom tracking) with telehealth services. These are fundamentally different monetization and delivery models — recurring SaaS/service vs. hardware unit sales."},"market":{"score":3,"rationale":"Both target consumers interested in health/wellness tracking, and Jawbone's UP fitness tracker touched on health data. However, the new pitch targets chronic-illness patients in Germany and France, while Jawbone was a US-based general consumer wellness hardware company. The clinical depth, geography, and user need are meaningfully different."},"gtm":{"score":2,"rationale":"Jawbone relied on retail distribution and hardware product launches to reach mainstream consumers. The new pitch would go direct-to-consumer digitally with a subscription app and telehealth, requiring entirely different GTM motions (app store presence, healthcare partnerships, potentially regulatory approval in the EU)."},"stage_scale":{"score":3,"rationale":"Both are consumer-facing health-adjacent ventures targeting scale, but Jawbone raised $900M+ and operated for 18 years as a hardware company. The new pitch appears to be an early-stage app startup. Scale ambitions are comparable only at the broadest level."}},"why_similar":"Both ventures sit in the consumer health-tracking space, aiming to give individuals visibility into their physical condition. Jawbone's UP tracker similarly promised data-driven personal health insights for everyday consumers — an early signal of the market appetite that the new pitch is building on. Both target non-clinical consumers rather than providers or payers, and both implicitly rely on high user engagement to deliver value.","where_diverged":"Several concrete dimensions diverge: (1) Geography — Jawbone was US-based; the new pitch targets Germany and France, bringing EU regulatory frameworks (GDPR, possible CE/MDR medical device classification) into play. (2) Monetization — Jawbone used one-time hardware purchase; the pitch uses monthly subscription recurring revenue. (3) Product type — Jawbone sold physical wearables; the pitch is a software app plus telehealth service with no hardware. (4) Customer segment — Jawbone targeted general wellness consumers; the pitch specifically serves chronic-illness patients, a higher-need, potentially more regulated segment. (5) Care delivery — the pitch includes on-demand telehealth, a service layer absent from Jawbone entirely.","failure_causes":["intense hardware competition from Apple Watch and Fitbit","chronic product quality and reliability issues (UP band defects)","inability to convert health data into defensible software moat","over-reliance on one-time purchase revenue with no recurring stream","capital inefficiency — $900M raised with no path to profitability","failure to pivot to clinical/health services before liquidation","supply chain and manufacturing cost pressures"],"lessons_for_input":["Build recurring subscription revenue from day one to avoid the revenue-cliff dynamic that plagued Jawbone's one-time hardware sales model.","Establish a defensible software and data moat early — symptom-tracking data for chronic-illness patients is clinically valuable; seek regulatory validation (e.g., CE mark as a digital health product) rather than avoiding it.","Target a specific, high-retention patient cohort (chronic illness) rather than the broad wellness market where Jawbone was commoditized; niche depth beats mass-market breadth at early stage.","Map EU regulatory requirements (GDPR, German DiGA reimbursement framework, French Mon Espace Santé) before scaling — potential reimbursement paths are also a distribution advantage unavailable to Jawbone.","Pair telehealth with outcomes data to demonstrate clinical efficacy; this creates switching costs and justifies subscription pricing in a way pure hardware never could."]}
